title: RFE: predictable port generation
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

One thing that is nice about Docker's port generation is that you can guess what it will be (sometimes). Basically, when you are launching container after container trying to get something to work, in docker, your generated port number will keep counting up. As a result, you can easily test by just changing the port number in the browser or on your command line from the last run. 

Podman, as far as I can tell, chooses pretty random port numbers. 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. launch apache container with "-P" and see port assigned (e.g. podman run -d -P httpd)
1. do it again
1. note that the ports generated are wildly different

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version
Version:            1.6.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.13.1
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-1.6.2-2.fc31.x86_64
```

comments.body: please explain how this helps users? the current algo is faster than counting imho so it would be a weighing of performance against user benefit.

I thought I had: being able to predict the port number makes the frustration of making a container work the first time lower because you don't have to look up the port every time you launch a container. 

Definitely a minor improvement but I recently went through this while trying to get something to work, ran through 20-30 containers and had to look up the port to change in the browser every time. With docker, I didn't have to look up the port which made one less step in the "code, build, test" cycle.

I would bet this is difficult from the nature of CNI.  Since each exec is different.
I would figure this would be fairly easy to script using the 

```
podman run -d -p 80 fedora sleep 300
6c2895e9b7e41845e62d1d5293afecda1ed86e3911f35d8d2ed9e5a0f5316ad8
$ podman inspect -l -f '{{ .NetworkSettings.Ports }}'
[{35751 80 tcp }]
```
A little additional awking and you get your port.
This issue had no activity for 30 days. In the absence of activity or the "do-not-close" label, the issue will be automatically closed within 7 days.
